C:
# Decision Memo: Build vs. Buy — Internal Analytics Dashboard

**Recommendation: Buy (adopt an off-the-shelf BI/analytics tool).** Build only the thin layer that connects it to our data. Reserve a custom build for later, and only if a specific need proves un-meetable by a vendor.

**Why buy.** An internal analytics dashboard is not a product differentiator — no customer chooses us because of how our internal charts look. The value is time-to-insight for our own team, and a mature tool (e.g., Metabase, Looker, Power BI, or a warehouse-native option) delivers that in days, not the months a credible in-house build takes. We'd also inherit maintenance we don't want: auth, permissions, chart rendering, export, mobile, and the long tail of "can you add this one filter" requests that quietly become a standing engineering cost.

**What buying actually costs.** Per-seat licensing that grows with headcount, less control over bespoke visualizations, and dependence on a vendor's roadmap and data-connector support. These are real but bounded, and most are cheaper to absorb than engineering salaries.

**When building would be right.** Only if one or more holds: the dashboard is itself a customer-facing feature we'd sell; our data model is so unusual that no tool can model it; compliance forbids data leaving our infrastructure and no self-hosted vendor qualifies; or per-seat costs at our projected scale exceed the fully-loaded cost of a small owning team. None of these appears to apply to a standard internal use case — but they're the trip-wires to check before committing.

**Suggested next step.** Run a one- to two-week paid pilot of two candidate tools against our real data sources and the three or four dashboards people actually ask for. Decide on time-to-first-useful-dashboard and connector fit. If both fail on a hard requirement, that failure is the business case for building — and we'll have the specifics to scope it.

---

I wrote this against a standard internal-tooling assumption since no company specifics were given. If you tell me the team size, budget, existing data stack, and whether this dashboard is ever customer-facing, I can tighten the recommendation and put real numbers behind the cost comparison. Want me to save this to your documents folder?
